checks.a_analysis: I have reviewed the entire JSON output against the source document. No fabricated paragraphs or sentences were found. The image alt text and metadata titles are within the scope of acceptable AI additions.

I verified all URLs, email addresses, and social media handles in the output (e.g., fcc.gov links, firsttech@nc.gov, it.nc.gov, @NCDIT) against the source document. All contact information present in the output is accurately sourced from the original document. No fabricated contact information was found.

The source document consists of 27 pages. The JSON output explicitly indicates that 19 sections were omitted (likely corresponding to pages 5-18, 20-22, 23, and 25). These pages contain significant content including broadband service inventory maps, adoption rates, barriers to deployment, and specific grant details that are not present in the output.
checks.b_evidence_source: The source document contains 27 pages of information, including maps (Pages 5, 11), barriers to adoption (Pages 10, 12), and state vision/plan details (Pages 14-18).
checks.b_evidence_location: Sections list, between elements 15 and 16 (index after section 4)

checks.a_analysis: The tables that were included in the output (Broadband Speeds on P3, Typical Broadband Usage on P4, and GREAT Grant Program on P24) have row counts that accurately reflect the data presented in the source document. For example, the GREAT Grant Program table in Section 24 correctly lists all 19 awards.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: There are two major omissions within sections that are otherwise present. First, Section 1 (Page 1) is missing the main presentation title 'What is Broadband?'. Second, Section 4 (Page 19) provides a table of regions but fails to include the accompanying contact names, phone numbers, and email addresses for the regional experts (Keith Conover, Jim Corrin, George T. Collier) which constitutes the primary data of that page.
checks.b_evidence_source: Page 19: 'West Keith Conover 828-450-7933 Keith.Conover@nc.gov', 'Central Jim Corrin 919-353-1126 Jim.Corrin@nc.gov', 'East George T. Collier 919-754-6558 George.Collier@nc.gov'
checks.b_evidence_output: The table in Section 4 only contains the region names (West, Central, East) without the corresponding contact details.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The text that has been successfully converted follows the source document accurately. No changes in wording that alter the meaning of the content were observed.

checks.a_analysis: I have reviewed the entire output JSON and compared it against the source PDF. The document contains various logos and branding elements (NC DIT Broadband Infrastructure Office) that repeat on almost every page in the original source. The pipeline has captured these as images within the respective sections. There are no instances of unique text blocks or paragraphs being erroneously duplicated in the output.
The heading hierarchy is generally consistent. Major page titles are assigned as H1, and subsections (such as on page 24) correctly use H2. No skips in levels (e.g., H1 to H3) were observed.

The table on page 3 ('Broadband Speeds') is structurally corrupted in the output. The header row defines a two-column structure ('Activity' and 'Minimum Speed'). However, the rows serving as category headers in the original PDF (e.g., 'General Usage', 'Watching Video') are rendered in the JSON as rows containing only a single cell. This creates a column count mismatch within the table object, breaking its integrity for screen readers.
checks.b_evidence_source: Table on page 3 containing categories like 'General Usage', 'Watching Video', 'Video Conferencing', and 'Gaming'.
checks.b_evidence_output: In section 3, the table rows corresponding to these categories (indices 1, 9, 13, 17 in the rows array) only contain one 'cells' object, unlike the other rows which contain two.

checks.a_analysis: The reading order follows the logical top-to-bottom, left-to-right flow of the presentation slides. Page titles precede body content, and legends follow the maps they describe (e.g., page 19).

On page 2 ('Broadband Technology Types'), the original document contains a detailed multi-level bulleted list. The output merges this entire list into a single paragraph element, losing the structural distinction between the list items and sub-items.
checks.b_evidence_source: Page 2 bulleted list starting with 'Digital Subscriber Line (DSL)' through 'Satellite'.
checks.b_evidence_output: "- Digital Subscriber Line (DSL)\n  - Runs over traditional copper phone lines..."

checks.a_analysis: Several pages in the source PDF contain clearly visible URLs and email addresses (e.g., pages 3, 4, 16, 26, 27). While the text for these links is preserved in the output, they have not been converted into functional link elements (hyperlinks), appearing only as plain text within paragraphs.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML output significantly changes the visual organization of several structured pages. Diagrams used to show relationships and process flow in the source are collapsed into simple lists. For example, on page 23, a flowchart with large arrows is converted into a series of bulleted text blocks. Similarly, the hexagonal groupings for 'Common Barriers' on pages 10 and 12 and the tiled layout for 'The Playbook' on page 21 are lost, fundamentally changing how the information is visually presented and understood.
checks.b_evidence_source: Page 23 shows 'Addressing the Homework Gap through Libraries' as a flowchart with distinct boxed sections connected by large downward arrows.
checks.b_evidence_output: The HTML renders the content of page 23 as a vertical stack of bulleted lists without any flowchart or diagrammatic structure.
checks.b_evidence_location: Addressing the Homework Gap through Libraries (Page 23)

checks.a_analysis: The tables present in the document are rendered correctly. The 'Broadband Speeds' table (page 3), 'Typical Broadband Usage' table (page 4), and the list of 'GREAT Awards' (page 24) are all converted into well-structured HTML tables that maintain the alignment of data into rows and columns, making them easy to read and understand.

Several critical and meaningful images are missing from the HTML output. This includes multiple maps of North Carolina (Broadband Service Inventory on p. 5, Adoption Rates on p. 11, and Technical Assistance Regions on p. 19). Additionally, the infographic sequence of house icons (pages 6-9), the images for 'The Playbook' tiles (p. 21), and the report cover image (p. 22) are absent. These graphics provide essential context and data visualization that is not fully captured by the remaining text.
checks.b_evidence_source: Page 5 shows the 'North Carolina Broadband Service Inventory' map showing coverage areas across the state.
checks.b_evidence_output: The HTML renders the title and legend text for the map, but the map image itself is missing.

checks.a_analysis: There are no instances of duplicated body content in the converted document. Each section, paragraph, and table from the source PDF appears only once in the body of the HTML output. The presence of headings in both the navigation/TOC area and the main body is expected and correct accessibility design.
